players.name: Zion Suzuki
players.bio: Born in New Jersey but raised for the Samurai Blue, Zion Suzuki is Japan's shot-stopper of the future. The Parma keeper made his senior debut as a teenager and is already Japan's first-choice. His World Cup 2026 is a homecoming of sorts — born on US soil.
players.facts: Born in Newark, NJ, Suzuki could technically have played for the USA — Japan's loss, America's missed opportunity.

players.name: Yukinari Sugawara
players.bio: An attack-minded right-back with an engine that never quits, Yukinari Sugawara has made the Bundesliga his playground at Werder Bremen. Two international goals from fullback underline his threat going forward. Still just 25, he's a key cog in Japan's fluid system.
players.facts: Sugawara's surname means 'field of sedge grass' — but there's nothing flat or boring about his overlapping runs.

players.name: Shōgo Taniguchi
players.bio: The veteran presence in Japan's backline, Shōgo Taniguchi brings experience and calm authority to defence. Now plying his trade at Sint-Truiden in Belgium, the 34-year-old has been a dependable servant across 38 caps. A reliable hand for a tournament of this magnitude.
players.facts: Taniguchi is one of the oldest outfield players in this Japan squad — a living bridge between generations of Samurai Blue.

players.name: Kō Itakura
players.bio: Commanding, technical, and utterly composed under pressure, Kō Itakura is Japan's defensive linchpin. Now at Ajax, the centre-back has shone across top European leagues and brings real leadership across 40 caps. Japan's backline stands or falls with him.
players.facts: Itakura was reportedly scouted and signed by Manchester City before being loaned around Europe to develop — quite the apprenticeship.

players.name: Yūto Nagatomo
players.bio: A legend in a left-back's boots, Yūto Nagatomo is one of the most-capped players in Japanese football history with 145 appearances. The 39-year-old's presence at a fourth World Cup would be the stuff of folklore. Still going at FC Tokyo, the man simply refuses to retire.
players.facts: Nagatomo has played at more World Cups than most players have international caps. He made his debut before some teammates were born.

players.name: Shuto Machino
players.bio: A powerful forward with an eye for goal, Shuto Machino has earned his stripes in the Bundesliga with Borussia Mönchengladbach. Five goals in 14 caps is a healthy return for a player still finding his international feet. Pace, physicality, and ambition — he's the full package.
players.facts: Machino made his Bundesliga mark in Germany — not bad for a forward who only broke through at the senior level relatively recently.

players.name: Ao Tanaka
players.bio: Ao Tanaka is the engine room Japan didn't know they needed until they had him. The Leeds United midfielder combines tireless pressing with genuine quality in possession, boasting 8 goals in 38 caps. One of the first names on the teamsheet — and rightly so.
players.facts: Tanaka's name 'Ao' means 'blue' in Japanese — perfectly fitting for a Samurai Blue midfielder who runs himself into the ground.

players.name: Takefusa Kubo
players.bio: Once dubbed 'Japan's Messi' as a La Masia graduate, Takefusa Kubo has done the hard work of living up to the hype at Real Sociedad. Skilful, direct, and capable of moments of magic, the winger is Japan's most exciting attacking talent. Expect fireworks in 2026.
players.facts: Kubo trained at Barcelona's La Masia academy as a child — then FIFA ruled it an illegal signing. Barcelona's loss, Japan's gain.

players.name: Keisuke Gotō
players.bio: Only 21 but already carrying the weight of future expectations, Keisuke Gotō is one of Japan's most exciting young forwards. Just four caps to his name, this World Cup is a massive stage — but talent this raw rarely stays quiet for long. Watch this space.
players.facts: Born in 2005, Gotō could become one of the youngest players ever to feature for Japan at a World Cup if called upon in 2026.

players.name: Ritsu Dōan
players.bio: Wearing the famous No.10 with complete authority, Ritsu Dōan is Japan's heartbeat. The Eintracht Frankfurt man scores goals from midfield, presses relentlessly, and delivers in big moments — as Germany 2022 proved. Eleven international goals and counting.
players.facts: Dōan scored against Germany in the iconic 2022 Qatar comeback — one of the World Cup's greatest upsets. No pressure repeating that.

players.name: Daizen Maeda
players.bio: Daizen Maeda is the human pressing machine Japan's system was built around. The Celtic forward's relentless work rate makes him every defender's nightmare — even if the goals don't always reflect his influence. Four caps and four smiles from manager Moriyasu.
players.facts: Maeda is so highly regarded at Celtic that fans sing his name to the tune of 'Hey Jude'. Not many Japanese players get a Beatles tribute.

players.name: Keisuke Ōsako
players.bio: Keisuke Ōsako is the trusted backup between the sticks, keeping Japan's No.1 honest in training. The Sanfrecce Hiroshima stopper has 11 caps to his name and is a reliable presence in the squad. Every great team needs a great second goalkeeper — Ōsako fits the bill.
players.facts: Ōsako has kept goal for Sanfrecce Hiroshima, one of Japan's most successful J.League clubs — a solid base for any keeper's career.

players.name: Keito Nakamura
players.bio: Raised in a Buddhist temple, Keito Nakamura brings a rare serenity and discipline to his game that belies his age. The Reims midfielder is a remarkable finisher — 10 goals in just 25 caps is a stunning return. Strict, smart, and seriously dangerous in front of goal.
players.facts: Nakamura grew up in a Buddhist temple, which perhaps explains why he's so calm when slotting in goals — inner peace, outward chaos.

players.name: Junya Itō
players.bio: Junya Itō is the speedster Japan's wide positions have been built around for years. The Genk winger has 15 international goals from 69 caps — a phenomenal record for a wide player. At 33, this could be his final World Cup, and he'll be desperate to sign off in style.
players.facts: Itō has spent most of his career in Belgium's Pro League — becoming one of Japan's most prolific wide players from an unlikely base.

players.name: Daichi Kamada
players.bio: A technically gifted midfielder who thrives in tight spaces, Daichi Kamada has made Crystal Palace his home after stints across Europe. Twelve goals in 49 caps is an impressive tally for a box-to-box player. One of Japan's most complete and reliable midfielders.
players.facts: Kamada previously played for Eintracht Frankfurt, where he was part of their remarkable 2022 UEFA Europa League winning squad.

players.name: Tsuyoshi Watanabe
players.bio: Solid, composed, and quietly effective, Tsuyoshi Watanabe has earned his place in Japan's defensive setup at Feyenoord. Eleven caps reflect a player still establishing himself at international level, but the talent is undeniable. He learns from the best in Rotterdam daily.
players.facts: Watanabe plays for Feyenoord alongside teammate Ayase Ueda — two Samurai Blue players at one of Europe's top clubs is quite the partnership.

players.name: Yuito Suzuki
players.bio: Yuito Suzuki is one of Japan's brightest young midfielders, taking his development to SC Freiburg in the Bundesliga. Just six caps so far, but he offers creativity and energy from central midfield. This World Cup could be the moment he truly announces himself.
players.facts: Suzuki shares his name with two other Japan squad members — distinguishing between the Suzukis in training must be a manager's nightmare.

players.name: Ayase Ueda
players.bio: Japan's most prolific active striker, Ayase Ueda has 16 goals in 39 caps — a ratio that demands respect. The Feyenoord forward is composed, clinical, and lethal in the box. In a Japan squad brimming with talent, Ueda is the man they look to when it matters most.
players.facts: Ueda is one of two Japan internationals at Feyenoord alongside Watanabe — the Rotterdam club has become something of a Samurai Blue hub.

players.name: Kōki Ogawa
players.bio: The statistics are staggering — 11 goals in just 15 caps makes Kōki Ogawa one of the most lethal strikers per game in Japanese football history. The NEC forward is an instinctive finisher with a gift for being in the right place at the right time. Don't blink.
players.facts: Ogawa averages nearly a goal every 1.4 games for Japan — a ratio that would make him the envy of most international strikers worldwide.

players.name: Ayumu Seko
players.bio: A composed and versatile defender now based at Le Havre, Ayumu Seko offers Japan tactical flexibility across the backline. Fourteen caps show a player growing into his international role steadily. He brings both reliability and an ability to adapt — invaluable in a tournament.
players.facts: Seko plays his club football in Le Havre, France — meaning he'll be very familiar with one of the host nation's football heartlands come 2026.

players.name: Hiroki Itō
players.bio: Playing for Bayern Munich is a statement of quality in itself, and Hiroki Itō has earned his place at European football's grandest club. The defender brings Bundesliga title-winning DNA to Japan's backline across 24 caps. Composure, pace, and quality — the complete package.
players.facts: Itō is one of very few Japanese players ever to represent Bayern Munich — joining an exclusive club both in Munich and in Japanese football history.

players.name: Takehiro Tomiyasu
players.bio: Takehiro Tomiyasu is the Swiss Army knife of Japan's defence — comfortable at right-back, centre-back, or wherever the team needs him. The Ajax defender has 43 caps and brings Premier League experience from Arsenal to the squad. Unflappable under pressure.
players.facts: Tomiyasu is fluent in English and Italian, having played in both countries — he may be Japan's most multilingual defender.

players.name: Tomoki Hayakawa
players.bio: Tomoki Hayakawa is the third-choice goalkeeper providing experienced domestic cover behind Japan's European-based stoppers. The Kashima Antlers stopper has four caps and keeps the squad's goalkeeping competition fierce. Every tournament needs reliable depth between the posts.
players.facts: Hayakawa keeps goal for Kashima Antlers — one of Japan's most successful clubs and birthplace of legends like Marquinhos Seisenbacher.

players.name: Kaishū Sano
players.bio: A technically astute midfielder who has earned his developmental stripes at Mainz 05 in the Bundesliga, Kaishū Sano is one of Japan's quieter but effective squad contributors. Thirteen caps and growing — the 25-year-old has the talent to make a real impact off the bench.
players.facts: Sano plays in the Bundesliga for Mainz 05, a club renowned for unearthing and developing talent — perfect breeding ground for a future Japan regular.

players.name: Junnosuke Suzuki
players.bio: At just 22, Junnosuke Suzuki is one of the youngest defenders in the squad and one of Japan's most exciting defensive prospects. The Copenhagen fullback has six caps and the kind of fearless attitude that thrives on the biggest stages. His time is very much now.
players.facts: Suzuki is one of three players named Suzuki in Japan's 2026 squad — the most common Japanese surname causing plenty of roll-call confusion.

players.name: Kento Shiogai
players.bio: One of the youngest players in the entire tournament, Kento Shiogai is only 21 but has earned a spot at VfL Wolfsburg and caught the eye of the national setup. Just two caps so far, but this World Cup appearance alone marks a remarkable rise for the young forward.
players.facts: Born in 2005, Shiogai may be one of the youngest players at the entire 2026 World Cup — a teenager-turned-professional making history.
